title: 2025/08/13 RR
displayTitle: Regulations Review Committee - Complaint about the Airports (Cost Recovery for Processing of International Travellers) Regulations 2025
topic: Complaint about the Airports (Cost Recovery for Processing of International Travellers) Regulations 2025
description: Complaint about the Airports (Cost Recovery for Processing of International Travellers) Regulations 2025 Dunedin International Airport Limited Daniel De Bono, Chief Executive Craig Shrive, Legal counsel - Russell McVeagh Ministry for Primary Industries Diane McDermott, Commissioner Central / South Stuart Anderson, DDG Biosecurity New Zealand Mary Craythrone, Manager Biosecurity System Policy
descriptionSummary: Complaint about the Airports (Cost Recovery for Processing of International Travellers) Regulations 2025. Dunedin International Airport Limited. Daniel De Bono, Chief Executive

chunks.text: Um, we are really interested in, uh, diving into some questions with you. Um, but if you could give us a sense of, um, what it is that, um, your role is and also the complaint that you've brought. Um, and then I'm sure we can dive right in. Brilliant. Don't need to hold this down to talk. Oh, no, No. Just use a big voice.

Kiara. Um, thank, thank you for the welcome. And actually thank you for the opportunity to speak, um, to the committee today. This is actually a really important issue, uh, that we're actually here today to deliver, um, a conversation with you. Um, I am Daniel, Deb Bono. I'm proudly the chief executive of Danita Airport, supported today by Craig Shri from who's our legal counsel on this issue.

So once again, thank you for the opportunity. Um, we'll take, um, our submission and accompanying materials as read. Um, but before we get into our conversations, a few key points I'd like to step through before we get to that point. Um, firstly for us, we've made this complaint because we believe the levies are simply unjust and unfairly targeted airports who have worked really hard to develop new international routes, we feel we've been penalized, um, for starting new services while established airports pay nothing

for border services on new routes. That's a key distinction here. Um, to need an airports served scheduled international services for 25 years up until COVID-19, COVID-19 saw, uh, significant consequential effects on the aviation sector, particularly the supply side shocks around fleet and crew availability, which has led to us taking a bit longer than some others to reestablish our footing on the international scene.

The, the airport sector was very united on its view that, um, international airport should be supported and border processing costs should be paid from existing levies. Those being the BPLs we're disappointed. The border agencies, um, didn't shift their view, um, from that because the airport cost recovery act existed and that because it existed, regulations had to be made.

The best solution here, in our view, should be repealing the act, although I appreciate that's not necessary from the committee's. Uh, powers, um, Love an ambitious, Ambitious, ambitious that, that's stuck with that thing. Um, let me start also with talking about the benefits of international services.

And we can't, I can't overstate this. Um, what we have done in Dunedin, uh, and what international services mean to communities is consistent with the tourism, the government's, uh, tourism growth roadmap. We're really grateful to have the support of Minister Upton and Minister Meger on the restart of our inaugural services last June back to the Gold Coast.

Um, great to have their support, um, the economic impact of what we've achieved in Indeed and delivers 58,000 more seats to New Zealand from Australia, which again, is consistent with that go for growth, um, agenda of the government. And actually Ford bookings are really good and we've had really strong demand on those services. They deliver direct visitor spend into our community and nothing, I tell you what, nothing will grow in economy faster than international service.

They deliver, uh, GST spend, uh, and revenue to the crown. And of course, they also deliver, uh, visitor spend to the regional and national economy, uh, and provide amenity and choice for parts of New Zealand in terms of international travel. The other advantage of direct international travel, of course, is that it reduces indirect, um, international travel, which, uh, in itself reduces greenhouse gas emissions associated from international aviation.

It's you burn less fuel flying directly to the point you want to go. If I go in directly, Can I stop you there for questions? Sure. Um, I've got two questions here. Perhaps while you think about it, I'll ask mine. So, um, committee's particularly interested in how those, um, BPL fees compare to the fee that you have been asked to pay.

Could you, um, compare for us the quantum, um, for you and then what, um, how the other airports have been effectively charged for this, but it's, it's spread in the other fees that they'll pay. Um, and why this fee that that's being against you is different from that.

Um, and whether this applies to anyone else, say perhaps Hamilton. Okay, let me, um, let me try and unpack that and answer this as eloquently as I can. Um, so as it sits now, we have the regime called the border processing levies. So the BPL is a standard national charge that is imposed on every single international air ticket.

So every single national international passenger pays via the airline, the levy to fund, uh, the costs for providing order services. Um, that regime still remains in Dunedin, so international visitors flying to Dunedin will still pay the VPL on their ticket. Additional, um, through this airport cost recovery Act is cost that are directly imposed on Dunedin Airport, not the customer for, um, reestablishment costs, um, to re to reestablish border processing services, if that makes sense.

Um, the other international imports, as they sit, um, they don't pay, uh, any cost directly for provision of border services. Those services and renewal of CapEx infrastructure at those airports is funded by the border processing levy. And, um, give us an idea of the amount, so what's levied, um, through BPL at the other bigger airports, if you know, and then what would be levied at Dunedin Airport through that BPL.

And then what additional charge are you being asked to front for here? So for Dunedin, um, I don't, I I can take that one on notice. The exact BPL fee is, it sits today, I don't have in front of me, but it's a standard charge that applies to everyone regardless of airport. Um, Which I imagine is very small for you compared to say Auckland. That's why I'm asking. It's the same. So when you No, But per Tickets. Per, yeah, per ticket, yeah. Yeah, per ticket's the same.

Whether you fly into Auckland or fly into Dunedin, Queenstown, Wellton, you pay the same amount on your ticket. Yeah, that's right. So it's a cross subsidized system by design, which actually works pretty well. Mm-hmm. Um, outside of that, the direct costs, um, imposed on Dunedin by border agencies, um, is a lump sum for actual costs incurred, um, by border agencies and reestablishing, uh, their presence at Dunedin, What is it Cost circa, circa half a million dollars as a a year?

No, to one off one. One off. Yep. Cool. And, um, I'll come to Grant McCullen or Miles Anderson. Miles. Miles, do you Have a question? Well, I sort of asked that of, um, NPI. Okay. Um, Um, the actual costs, um, that is something that this committee delves into regularly.

Um, do you think that the fee that's being, um, proposed to you reflects the actual cost of, um, what is, you know, the service offering that you are getting for it? I would say the actual cost, yes. Whether they're reasonable is still to be determined. So we are going through, uh, line by line, um, the cost recovery invoicing and what they're passing on to us.

There are a few questions we need to step through with that at this point, but we haven't yet, um, come back. But our principal view, uh, is that they, they shouldn't exist because the BPL system is a mechanism to recover, um, cost fund services at the border. I just, I wanna understand, so you, the only reason you stopped taking 'cause of COVID right?

They shut down to that point? Yes. Right. And did any other airports, international airports in New Zealand shut down at that point that you're Aware of? Correct. Great question. Um, so, uh, Wellington and Queensland Airports also ceased international, scheduled international Air Operations at the same time. Mm-hmm. Um, for those two airports, they were without a scheduled flight for over a year.

Mm-hmm. We took four more years to get our services back. Um, but, um, these regulations weren't created under haste, um, to seek recovery of reestablishment costs for those airports. But they have been for us And, and, and the other recently also in chips.

Right. Um, Hamilton reestablished as an international airport, do you know how that's been treated? I believe, um, I don't wanna speak on Hamilton or Waikato airport's behalf, but I believe, um, a similar arrangement to this. Um, but I don't have particular details around the Waikato example.

The one thing I will state here, though, is a quite different situation. Please explain. Yeah. The white catto, um, is a restart for a pretty long absence. That's right. That's right. Whereas Dunedin, we had, we, we were forced to close with the border and the COVID-19 impacts, and that led to Virgin Australia also going to, um, receivership or administration in Australia.

And then of course, the supply side shock that perpetuated after in, in terms of re reestablishing services. But the key point for me, um, and for this committee is we had services for 25 years continuously. It wasn't a stop start, stop start. There was no fiscal risk to the crown over that period. The services were sustainable. One final question. So why did it take you so long to get restarted?

That's another great question. Um, reflecting on my comments about supply side shock as, as the system recovered from COVID-19, there was a massive shortage of crew. Mm-hmm. And then of course, around the world in terms of engineering parts, equipment, all the rest of it that led to shortages of, of aviation capacity to actually redeliver connectivity.

So that, that in itself created challenges for us. We are in a global marketplace, um, trying to work with airlines and reestablishing connectivity. They're limited with what they have available, uh, redeploying capacity, um, in a kind of sequential manner. Um, so it took us a bit longer to get there, but we, we finally got there. Thank you. Just another couple of questions on the timing point that grants raised.

Um, these, uh, this sort of cost recovery as a lump sum wasn't in operation before, um, the pandemic period, was it? Uh, no. So why do you think it was introduced now? Why do you think this, um, do you have insight into why, um, the fee is being levied in this way over and above the BPL charges that paid for this before?

It's something I, we really struggle to wrap our head around because the BPL mechanism works. It is, it is the way to recover these costs, um, if, if at all. So, um, my, my only, my only thought was to be frank, is that the potential for other regionally ports to decide to start international services presented some challenges for agencies around costs and appropriations, which has led to officials looking for a mechanism to try and recover, um, additional costs.

And did you anticipate this sort of charge? Like was there warning, what, what did you know before it was, was there constipation with you? What did that Look like? So they did commence consultation with us in the middle of last year, right. At the point about noon. Yeah. And right at the point we're also signing a deal with Qantas, the Qantas group, for our new Jetstar services. So, um, even at that point, the quantum of costs expected for reestablishment were not known to us.

We didn't know about the costs and the particulars of those until the start 2025. Right. So this was after the services were already signed. Yes. Um, So the, the, the bill approximately half a million dollars is Holland. What sort of impact will that have on Canadian Airport? Um, another great question. So, um, I might, I might talk a little bit about international aviation route development for most airports.

And we are no different. It's a zero sum game. So the first few years of establishing a new international air route, um, there's, there's nothing in it for an airport company. Um, it's a risk share arrangement. Um, so for us, it's, this isn't about the bottom line and the short term, it is in the long term in terms of, um, building that sustainable aviation connectivity.

Um, but, um, there's nothing left in the tank. So when we go into these negotiations with an airline like any other airport, you are putting your best foot forward and working through, uh, a business case for them to put capacity to your airport versus another. Yep. Um, and you, you carefully balance all those competing, uh, kind of priorities in that process.

I'll come back to your council to see if, um, there's any comments, but I just wanna ask our council if there's anything we have missed in our fact finding that we talked about. No, That's good. We're good. Okay. Um, Craig, do you wanna, oh, you have question? One question. So one of, one of the letters that we have, um, in front of us actually states that, um, there was a letter that came through and the cost was approximately 120,000 to 140,000.

Do you know why it went up to 500,000 from that approximate cost? I don't have that letter knowledge of that in front of me, but happy to take that one on notice. It was a letter from Dunedin Airport through Christine Stevenson. We're happy to receive some more, um, you know, an, uh, fuller answers to our questions if you'd like to provide them after the hearing.

Yeah, thank you. That'll be fine. Yeah. Great. We are out of time. Um, is there anything short in closing you'd like to share with us? Anything we've missed that you want us to focus on? Um, uh, one last point would be about, um, the airport Cost Recovery Act versus the mechanisms that now exist within the customs and the biosecurity acts for cost recovery.

I think, I think what we are, what we're dealing with at the moment is the fact that the airport cost recovery, cost recovery Act still exists, albeit we believe it's defunct. Um, has, has provided this opportunity for agencies to use that, um, piece of legislation to seek cost recovery outside of the BPLs, the BPLs, um, as we see it can recover the costs for reestablishing services and intentionally airports.

Yep. Yep. Would you recommend the committee, um, seek evidence from any other parties that have an interest in this? Not that I can think of, but Greg, uh, New Zealand airport. Yeah. Does New Zealand airports agree with you? Yes. I mean, I think further evidence sought from New Zealand Airports Association would be helpful for this committee.

Um, okay. And, and evaluating this issue. Great. Yeah. Thank you so much. Thank you. Thank you. You are very welcome to stay. This is public session. Okay, Thank you. Um, next we have from customs in the Ministry of Primary Industry, our friends joining us at the table.

Welcome. Please, before you get started, just give us a sense of who's here we'll be. Oh, thank you. Um, thank you. I, Stuart Anderson. I'm the Deputy Director general of Biosecurity New Zealand at MPI. Um, Mary Chor is our, uh, policy manager from NPI as well.

Uh, got Dan McDermott, who's our commissioner, uh, for looks after biosecurity for the central South. Uh, so Danita airport operations fall under her. And we also have, um, others from the MPR team and customs, uh, with us as well. Just Pop your hands up which ones? Oh, yeah. Great. Thank you. Um, over to you.

I'm sure you can, uh, get a sense of where the questions are, but I want to hand over for a few opening comments to give a perspective. Yes. Yeah. Thank you. Yeah, I'll just try a quick, uh, few introductory comments and of course questions. Um, so, uh, in, in terms of our position on this is that the airport cost recovery regulations, um, do not breach the standing orders, and they don't need to be drawn to the house's attention, the regulations as structured to meet the intent of the cost recovery Airports Cost Recovery Act.

The objectives are obviously to reduce the crown's exposure to unpredictable liabilities when international airports start or restart, and ensure that airports actually factor these costs into their operations and, and, and their business decisions. Um, if Parliament had considered that the airport sectors historical defunct, um, it could have repealed that when, when enacting the, uh, border processing levies in 2015.

Um, but that didn't happen. And, um, the ground rules were actually set out as to how the two act should operate. Uh, there is no double recovery of costs occurring. Uh, the Biosecurity Act, the Customs and Excise Act explicitly prohibit cost double double recovery of costs. So neither us nor customs, uh, are recovering costs twice under this regime.

Um, the regulations recover costs only under section seven, and that is, that is focused on establishing capacity for processing travelers. The actual costs of traveler processing, once things are up and running, then get recovered under the border processing levels. Um, the regulations do also impose a strict discipline around cost recovery.

So establishment costs are clearly defined as to what can and can't be recovered. Uh, so things like physical alterations, search benches, x-ray machines, and so on cabinet, and making these decisions also directed us to act in good faith, uh, work with the airports on reasonable grounds, uh, for the actual costs.

We do believe that we've done so. Um, in the case of Dun Airport and Hamilton, uh, the agencies have worked really hard to, to bring the cost down as far as possible, uh, and reduced them significantly from where we started with this. Um, the regulations are consistent with those cost recovery principles of that the costs should be charged to those, uh, who benefit from or create the need for the services, and that commercial enterprises shouldn't be subsidized, um, and should factor in those costs.

Um, using the border processing lever to pay for establishment or restart costs would remove those incentives, uh, defeating the policy intent behind the legislation. And then just lastly, um, in terms of the apportionment of costs, uh, and that, that question of how we recover them, both ourselves and customs, um, have really robust wide established frameworks around how we collect costs and how we, uh, manage that.

The accounting of those we're transparent in the charges and what we and what we collect. Um, processing levies, passenger levies, border levies are ring-fenced in our account system. Um, they're reported annually and that watershed on, uh, so we are, we are confident around, around these measures and that the regulations aligned with the intent and the acts.

Thank you. That's very helpful. I've got a question here and I'll take one. Yep. Um, one simple question. What, what did you charge Queenstown and Wellington when they restarted? So, so, so, Queenstown, Wellington, Auckland, Christchurch, uh, weren't charged reestablishment or restart costs. Um, they, they started operations, uh, as soon as the borders reopened following COVID, uh, there was also nothing required to, to reestablish those.

So essentially, uh, flights ended, uh, certainly in, um, in, uh, when Auckland had flights through COVID. Yeah. But, uh, equipment wasn't, uh, the airports were not shut down in terms of, um, uh, equipment removed, uh, gear fit out, uh, which, which was required for Dunedin and Hamilton to actually set things up to start processing passengers.

So why then? Okay. That's an interesting point you made there. So obviously, okay. Because of the shorter close shutdown period In the period of the period of time following Borders reopening. Uh, So what happened? Did you remove things from Dunedin, the capital equipment that was down there? Did you remove that and then have to reestablish it?

Yep. You, I can, I can talk to that. Um, there was some equipment start left in Dunedin, however, equipment has changed over time as well, upgraded it, et cetera. Some of the equipment was no longer usable, so we refurbished where we could. I think the original cost that we estimated for Dunedin was 276,000.

We brought that down to about 115. Um, and all the stuff that was reusable, we did reuse it where we could. That's helpful. Um, my question was going to be about, um, trying to understand this, um, that, that real cost recovery, um, let me also, uh, get a better sense of the timing decisions here.

So, um, the, the reason for bringing in, um, these, these provisions now, um, seems to be where any or any unusualness might arise. Um, so walk us through the thinking around, um, why the sort of more generalized BPL shouldn't exist, um, to recover those costs over time and why.

Um, it's this point in time where there should be a lump sum payment, because obviously you could set BPL, so it would be you've had this emission across subsidized system, um, or you could, and, and that would recover over time from all of the airports with international travel travel, or you could use this system where you have, um, essentially lumpy expenditure for an airport, but not lumpy expenditure for the government.

Um, so let Mary expand on this, but I think look, uh, broadly. Um, so, um, the, the border processing levies, uh, actually, uh, cannot be used for establishment costs or restarting for airports. Uh, and the provisions, why Were they used then for Queenstown? They're Used for processing passengers, so, so once the airport is up and running, uh, the passengers pay their levy on, on their airfare that pays for the processing costs of passengers coming through airports.

I'll just stop you there. I, I wonder if you've made a mistake, why were they then used for hap for Queenstown in those airports that restarted after COVID without any charges? Um, so there was no, there was no, there was no establishment cost involved with those airports closed because of COVID. And when, when, when the borders opened, uh, the airports continued operating.

So, so there was, there, well there wasn't a, there wasn't an establishment or set up cost required to get those airports up and running to accommodate passengers once the border is opened following COVID, how Dun Dunedin required put out recoding new equipment to be purchased to push into the airport to set it up and operated.

Is that, is that different to Dunedin? Yes, Yes. Yes. In the period of time from when the border has reopened to the need an opening this year in 2025, Help, help us to understand why. Do you wanna talk? Yeah, sure. Um, so we've got two different types of costs that we are recovering at the moment.

One is the process and costs, which is once a airport is up and running, um, all the costs go through those border processing levies. And that's a well established process now since 2015. So the costs we are talking about of the airport's Cost Recovery Act are establishment costs. So they're separate from that. Um, if there's no establishment costs, then we don't need to go there in terms of using regulations under the Airport's Cost Recovery Act, um, the Airports Cost Recovery Act also has grace period of six months within where if you have to stop operating,

then you can go back in again. Um, certainly COVID was, well, clearly like nothing we've ever seen before. Um, it meant that in some respects, all bets were off and we were trying our very best to make sure that we were doing the best for our border services. Um, and as Stu and Diana have said, we didn't need to go there because there weren't any establishment costs, Dunedin was different.

Um, the time period between them stopping and now restarting in this June, um, drove costs and drove establishment costs. Diana's teams worked really hard with Dunedin Airport to make sure we've minimized those. And, um, Steiner said, we've brought them down from, what was it, two 70 to about one 50, um, noting customs costs will be invoiced separately to that. Okay.

Thank you. I've got a question here. So I have a process question. So do you have an agreement between NPI and Dunedin Airport where you actually sit down, have a meeting and agree which equipments you would actually renew? So that Dunedin Airport actually has a say because it is a cost recovery, um, model, and it's not a decision that you just make on your own or customs makes on their own as to which equipment to get rid of and which one to just bind you. Yeah, So Diane can talk to the process, process around that can talk.

Um, I mean, ultimately we, you know, we do need to make the actual decisions in terms of what we need to run the facility and operate. Um, but, um, but we'll obviously work with Dun Airport and, and Hamilton, uh, in terms of getting there. And that's part of the process that's been followed to get this, this cost down from where we started, uh, from 270, 280 odd thousand down to about 115. But, um, I can talk to that.

A, a typical example would be the IT equipment, for instance, laptops, et cetera. They get defunct very, very easily. They run, they come to end of life, so we have to have laptops to operate. But we, we sat down with Dunedin for several months explaining where our costs were, what equipment we'd need to bring in. There's some equipment that we've minimized that we might have in other airports that we don't quite have in Dunedin.

We've gone for a cheaper, um, version. So we've tried to do everything we possibly can to minimize the amount of equipment that had to be purchased. Another example will be signage and postage, et cetera. So layouts, et cetera, had changed. We've used all the signage that was there as much as we possibly can, but we had to add some minimal signage to assist.

So we did sit down with Edin for several months, but we do reserve the right to be able to say, we require this in order to operate. So there was no equipment discarded as such. It was just equipment needed to be brought in. That's helpful. Thank you. Um, I'm gonna have three more questions. We've got four more minutes. Dr. Nan first. Oh, okay.

Miles Anderson, then Grant McCullum, then Dr. Nan, then we'll come to council. Just wanna Talk about a couple of invoices that, um, you need an airport as a chain should be, um, covered by border processing duties and not the one off cost. One was around, um, training and recruitment costs, and the other was for, uh, x-ray role of Gravity system.

Yeah. Mm-hmm. Um, could you in enlighten us on those please? So, Training costs for renewing established airport, we have to recruit additional staff. What we've done to minimize that cost to Dunedin Airport as well, is we've actually taken staff from across the country and put them in. So there's actually very little training costs being applied, if any, to need airport through the invoice.

But you don't agree that it should be paid for by the board of process Levy as opposed to the Not for establishment Establishment cost. And what about the x-ray roll gravity system again, is that, uh, you, you are, you are saying it's establishment cost? Yes. The x-ray that was present in the red airport is nearly end of life, really old.

We had it refurbished, put the roller system in, and again, we did everything we could to make sure that was a minimal cost roller system. And the bench attached it, for instance, is not our normal roller system. It's a cheaper single bench. Um, so we did minimize that as much as possible, but we do need the equipment Grant McCallum. Yeah, I'm, I'm a pretty female minded person, and COVID was a very unique experience for everybody.

And Queenstown and Wellington are much bigger airports. Right. And, and therefore you can see how they could get back going a lot quicker to need in a much smaller airport, and they're being punished. It would appear to me by simply because of the global situation they came across as a small player in trying to get reestablished, they're getting punished, whereas the old, which whereas the bigger airports are just sort of were able to carry on.

And it just strikes me as being completely unfair. Would you have any comment on that? Well, again, I think I'd, I'd come back to, you know, the objectives of the act, uh, in the airport Cost Recovery Act, which are to reduce the crown's exposure to liabilities. And so ministers and cabinet have decided to put these regulations in place with, with one of those objectives in mind.

Um, in that, uh, new airports starting up or reestablishing, uh, under the airport cost recovery Act need to pay the cost of those establishment. Yeah. So, so do you, did you go, did you approach cabinet to see if you could get some capital expenditure for this? No. Well, no. Well, ministers obviously, uh, go through cabinet.

Um, but we were faced with the situation. We sually, there were two new airports looking to, to set up reestablish, um, and, uh, and that, you know, that, that, that brought the matter to light and, um, hence the process has been followed to put these regulations into place. Dr. Thank you. Uh, one of the things we just heard, again, it's a process and may a timeline question, apologies, this has been responded to before, um, which is the hospital said that, have, they were in negotiation airport, Did I say Have hospital? Hospital?

Oh, the hospital airport. The need airport, um, said that they were in negotiations with, um, Jetstar. And again, it wasn't until after that kind of contract contractual agreement has been signed that they again found out exactly how much they're gonna be charged. Mm-hmm. From a process perspective, what's the process of you working with the Nan Hospital to ensure that that cost at least an estimated cost is upfront before some of those contractual decisions?

Mm-hmm. Yeah, there's two aspects of that. One is, um, knowing what the actual quantum of the costs were. And the other thing is knowing that this is a business cost that you could reasonably and foreseeably expect. So the Airport's Cost Recovery Act has been on the box since 2014. So it is well known that this is a liability that a new restarting airport could be liable for.

It's the first point. Um, the second point is that, um, there's been informal discussions with Dunedin Airport for some time. Um, and even before we started consulting on this, um, Dunedin airport did know that it was possible that they could be liable for these costs. Then we went through a process of formal consultation through our discussion document.

And, um, I'm not quite sure if the exact timeline, Daniel, as to when the paperwork was signed, but our view is this is a foreseeable business cost. Fair enough. They didn't know the exact quantum that, that that is true. And that's partly because we needed to get those regs in place to enable, to know what was the parameters of the regulations, which then drives the costs.

Thank you. Now, ministers have been really clear with us that they expected us to work in good faith, and we've done that. Thank you. Um, and just one final question and then we'll, um, thank you very much for your time. The, um, just what you've said there, that is I think a key point. Mm-hmm. When was the last time that the Airports Recovery, uh, airport Cost Recovery Act was used to charge a lumps lump sum?

So this, this is the first time, 'cause we needed the regulations in place in order to fully implement the act. And when were the regulations in place? Um, since, uh, 10 March, 2025. Okay. Thank you very much. This is an important part of, um, accountability of executive government. For the people who follow along on this closely, there won't be many, but thank you very much for coming, outing our questions.
